content.full_text:
Re
Fern Pourrcal PnacrrcEs CouvrrssroN
428 J Street o Suite 620 o Sacramento, CA 95814-2329
(916) 322-5660 . Fax (916) 322-0886
November 19,2003
Mark W. Steres, Asst. City Attorney
City of Monterey Park
c/o Brown, Winfield &Canzonei
300 South Grand Avenue, 15ft Floor
Los Angeles, CA 90071-3125
Your Request for Reconsideration of Advice
Our FiIe No. A-03-221
Dear Mr. Steres:
You have asked for reconsideration of advice provided to you in Steres Advice
Letter, No. 4-03-155, on behalf of Councilmember Sharon Martinezregarding the
conflict-of-interest provisions of the Political Reform Act (the'.Act,).r
We previously advised and reaffirm that Councilmember Martinez must
disqualifu herself from decisions regarding the improvements and modifications of the
easements acquired by the Monterey Park Redevelopment Agency because her source of
income, Southern California Edison ("Edison') retains a property interest in the
easements in question. Facts about CouncilmemberMartinez's economic interest in
Edison and the easements are describedin Steres Advice Letter, No. A-03-155. you
have not submitted any additional facts for consideration.
You argue that Edison should be determined to be indirectly involved in the
decisions pertaining to improvanents and modifications of the easements acquired by the
redevelopment agency based on the definition of "subject of a proceeding" as defined at
section l870a.l@)(2). You submit that since Edison has sold the easemint over the
land, that the owner of the easement, the redevelopment agency, will be the...subject, of
any permit or entitlement decision, not Edison's property." You support this by defining
an easement as "a legal and possessory interest in land of another, by which the owner of
the easement is entitled to make use of the land." You also state that "Edison's interest in
decisions relating to the easernent is no different than its interest in decisions relating to
the abutting shopping center site." The nature of an easement, as seen in the definition
you have provided, is a right to use the space in question; the owner of the space,
I Government Code sections 81000 - g1}l4. Commission regulations appear at Title 2, sections
18109-18997, of the Califomia Code of Regulations.


File No. A-03-221
Page No. 2
however, retains an interest in the property.2 Applying this definition to the case at hand,
the redevelopment agency has an interest in real property because of the easement and
Edison retains an interest in the real property as well. In fact, despite the granting of an
easement, Edison may still make any use of the land which does not interfere
unreasonably with the easement. (Camp Meeker W'ater System, Inc. y. Public Utiltties
Commission (1990) 51 Cal.3d 845; City of Pasadena v. California-Michigan Land &
Water Company (1941) 17 Cal.2d 576.) Also, for easements founded upon a grant, as
appears to be the case here, "only those interests expressed in the grant and those
necessarily incident thereto pass from the owner of the fee." (Pasadena v. California-
Mtchigan etc. Co., supra, I7 Cal.2d atp. 579.) In this case, Edison not only retains a
basic property interest but also has ultimate design approval over easement improvements
and grading plans for all easement parcels. For purposes of the Act, Edison has retained
a property interest in the easement and is directly involved in the decision regarding use
of the easernent property.3
If you have any other questions regarding this matter,please contact me at (916)
322-5660.
Sincerely,
Luisa Menchaca
General Counsel
By:
Galena West
Counsel, Legal Divi sion
Gwje
I : \Adviceltrs\A-03 -22 1
2 Black's Law Dictionary defines an easement as: "A right of use over the property of another,"
and,"Aninterestwhichonepersonhasinthelandofanother." 
(SeeBlack'sLawDict.(6thed. 1991)p.
352, col.2.)
3 According to your facts, the decision in question will determine improvements or modifications
to the easement property acquired from Edison by the redevelopment agency. In addition to the other
rights preserved by Ediso4 Edison also possesses the right to enjoin any use of the easement that materially
increases the burden on the property or imposes a new and additional burden. (Walt v. Rudotph (1961) 198
Cal.App.2d 684.) If these improvements or modifications go beyond reasonable use of the easement,
Edison could be able to prevent enforcement of any decision made.

sections.conclusion: We previously advised and reaffirm that Councilmember Martinez must disqualifu herself from decisions regarding the improvements and modifications of the easements acquired by the Monterey Park Redevelopment Agency because her source of income, Southern California Edison ('Edison') retains a property interest in the easements in question.
sections.question_synthetic: Whether Councilmember Martinez must disqualify herself from decisions regarding improvements and modifications of easements acquired by the Monterey Park Redevelopment Agency when her employer, Southern California Edison, retains a property interest in those easements.
sections.conclusion_synthetic: Councilmember Martinez must disqualify herself from decisions regarding easement improvements because Edison retains a direct property interest in the easements and is directly involved in decisions regarding their use, creating a conflict of interest under the Political Reform Act.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: This is a reconsideration letter responding to a request to reconsider previous advice (A-03-155). The document lacks traditional 'QUESTION' and 'CONCLUSION' section headers but contains clear question and conclusion content. Citation format and legal analysis structure are typical of FPPC advice letters.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: Whether Councilmember Martinez must disqualify herself from decisions regarding improvements and modifications of easements acquired by the Monterey Park Redevelopment Agency when her employer, Southern California Edison, retains a property interest in those easements.

CONCLUSION: We previously advised and reaffirm that Councilmember Martinez must disqualifu herself from decisions regarding the improvements and modifications of the easements acquired by the Monterey Park Redevelopment Agency because her source of income, Southern California Edison ('Edison') retains a property interest in the easements in question.

embedding.summary: The FPPC reaffirms its previous advice that Councilmember Martinez must disqualify herself from redevelopment agency decisions on easement modifications because her employer Edison retains a property interest in the easements. The FPPC rejects the argument that Edison's interest is indirect, holding that Edison retains both a basic property interest and design approval rights over easement improvements.
